channel.title: # 🇱🇰 This Week in Sri Lanka
channel.description: Generated by gpt-5 from 161 English News Articles published between 2026-04-29 and 2026-05-06.

item.title: The Government tabled a Bill to amend the Value Added Tax Act, widening the base and taxing cross‑border digital services.
item.description: The measure cut the registration threshold to Rs. 36 million a year and Rs. 9 million per taxable period from 1 July, drawing more small and wholesale or retail firms into the net. Non‑resident online providers must register and charge tax on supplies to users in the country, with business‑to‑business transactions excluded where documentation is in place. The levy on financial intermediation rises to 20.5% from July, while all registrants must adopt secured Point‑of‑Sale (POS, electronic checkout) systems within three months of a prescribed date. Penalties increase to a maximum Rs. 1 million, schedules can be filed progressively within a period, and zero‑rating is clarified for buying‑office services paid in foreign currency.

item.title: Sri Lanka and the Maldives exchanged seven cooperation agreements during a two‑day State visit to Colombo.
item.description: The documents covered tourism, archives, teacher training, youth and sports, counter‑terrorism, defence education and medical collaboration. Land allocations were agreed in the capitals for each other’s diplomatic missions, while a business forum was scheduled to deepen trade and investment links. The island nation is the third‑largest South Asian export market for its neighbour and invited investors to expand in information technology, fisheries processing, real estate and leisure. The visiting delegation also explored the scope for a bank presence and premium seafood distribution in the local market.

item.title: A parliamentary finance committee was told a new insolvency law could unlock over Rs. 30 billion in distressed deals.
item.description: The Committee on Public Finance (CoPF, a legislative budget oversight panel) heard that more than $100 million of capital is lined up, with hotel assets prominent, but needs a predictable, time‑bound process. The draft replaces liquidation‑first rules with court‑supervised moratoria and creditor‑approved plans that bind all classes if thresholds are met. Stakeholders flagged gaps on tax treatment and the absence of a Limited Liability Partnership (LLP, a pass‑through investment vehicle) regime to structure funds onshore. Concerns included court capacity, timelines, and alignment with existing enforcement, especially for small firms facing faster recovery action than large exposures.

item.title: The Asian Development Bank signalled a strategic shift and began assessing a request for budget financing support.
item.description: The institution said the proposal was under internal consideration and framed in a way that could channel funds into energy and food security as well as the budget. It also launched a $70 billion connectivity initiative targeting integrated power grids and digital infrastructure, with a goal of about 20 gigawatts (GW, a billion watts) of cross‑border renewable capacity integrated by 2035. Short‑term tools such as trade and supply‑chain finance and fast‑disbursing assistance were prioritised to cushion shocks from the Middle East war. A medium‑term focus on resilience was outlined as energy prices and shipping disruptions raised inflation and fiscal pressures across developing Asia.

item.title: Cabinet approved rules expanding the Central Bank’s powers to collect statistics and impose administrative sanctions.
item.description: The measure, issued by Extraordinary Gazette on 12 March, set out reporting scopes, formats, confidentiality protections and penalties for non‑compliance. It is being submitted to Parliament alongside the institution’s audited 2025 financial statements and operational report for legislative oversight. The framework draws on the Central Bank of Sri Lanka Act No. 16 of 2023 and is intended to strengthen data for monetary and supervisory functions. Administrative actions are available against entities that fail to provide required information within prescribed timelines.

item.title: Construction activity stayed in expansion in March, though momentum eased from February’s pace.
item.description: The Purchasing Managers’ Index for Construction (PMI, a diffusion gauge where 50 marks no change) showed Total Activity at 57.1 compared to 70.3 a month earlier. Firms reported continued growth in new orders and hiring, with the Employment index at 57.1 and quantities purchased also higher. Suppliers’ delivery times lengthened markedly as transport delays increased, while some respondents built up stocks on expectations of price rises. The compiler noted that supply constraints and logistics bottlenecks persisted even as project pipelines remained supportive.

item.title: Authorities convened a high‑level review to roll out suburban electric rail alongside the existing network.
item.description: The first services are planned to start early next year on three corridors, including the Kelani Valley line from Maradana to Makumbura. The initial package also targets the main route from the coastal business district to Ragama, and the seaside corridor from the central station to Panadura. Officials examined funding and technical options, including a potential shift from broad to standard gauge (track width used in many global systems). Upgrades to signalling and power systems formed part of the modernisation map discussed at the Presidential Secretariat.

item.title: A private lender completed the transfer of a foreign bank’s retail portfolio, adding about 200,000 customer accounts.
item.description: The transaction took effect on 1 May and expanded the local branch network to 96 locations, including seven outlets acquired in urban and regional centres. Four of the incoming sites were designated as dedicated private banking centres, and staff associated with the portfolio were onboarded. Products spanning savings, current accounts, fixed deposits, cards and consumer loans were integrated under the receiving institution’s platforms. Extended branch hours and helpline support were offered during the transition to minimise disruption for retail clients.

item.title: Police arrested more than 120 foreign nationals in a raid on an apartment complex tied to alleged financial fraud.
item.description: Investigators seized laptops and mobile phones for forensic analysis and said several suspects fled during the operation in Rajagiriya. The group, which included persons from multiple Asian and African countries, was alleged to have coordinated illegal transactions across several floors of the building. Local officers said follow‑up searches were underway to locate those who escaped. Preliminary findings indicated a large‑scale scam operation that had targeted victims through digital channels.

item.title: A press‑freedom index recorded a modest rise for the country to 134th place with a score of 40.77.
item.description: The report cited lingering concerns over statutory tools that can restrict reporting, including the Press Council law and the Online Safety Commission established in 2024. It also highlighted instances where the ICCPR Act (International Covenant on Civil and Political Rights enabling statute) and national‑security provisions had been used against journalists and dissenting voices. The five‑place improvement from the prior year came against a global backdrop of worsening conditions for reporters. The survey of 180 jurisdictions classed more than half as "difficult" or "very serious" environments for media work.

item.title: The Asian nation sent its largest‑ever delegation of 14 business leaders to a United States investment summit.
item.description: The group attended the SelectUSA forum to build networks and explore entry into healthcare, manufacturing and digital‑services markets. Organisers said the mission aimed to deepen commercial partnerships and attract capital into export‑oriented ventures. The programme connected firms with regional development bodies and potential partners across multiple states. The summit is run by the U.S. Department of Commerce to promote foreign direct investment into the American economy.
